coredata.dc:title: Evaluation on corrosion mitigation capabilities of nitrogen doped carbon dots as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in descaling solution
coredata.dc:creator: Saraswat, Vandana
Sarkar, Tarun Kanti
Yadav, Mahendra
coredata.dc:description: The article represents a mechanistic and detailed evaluation of synthesized novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in 15 % HCl solution. The investigation reflects the synergistic adsorption and corrosion inhibition capabilities of N-CDs. The investigation reflects that the N-CDs display efficient corrosion inhibition of 93.09 % efficiency for a dosage of 350 ppm. The N-CDs adsorb upon the metallic substrate surface to generate a protective layer at the metal solution interface and thereby protect it from the aggressive electrolyte. The electrochemical analyses confirm that the N-CDs behave as mixed type corrosion inhibitors and protect both anodic and cathodic sites of corrosion. The protective film was chemically analyzed through XPS analysis which confirms the inorganic/organic, hybrid nature of the protective film.

Langmuir isotherm
Mixed type corrosion inhibitors

head.ce:title: Evaluation on corrosion mitigation capabilities of nitrogen doped carbon dots as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in descaling solution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The article represents a mechanistic and detailed evaluation of synthesized novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in 15 % HCl solution. The investigation reflects the synergistic adsorption and corrosion inhibition capabilities of N-CDs. The investigation reflects that the N-CDs display efficient corrosion inhibition of 93.09 % efficiency for a dosage of 350 ppm. The N-CDs adsorb upon the metallic substrate surface to generate a protective layer at the metal solution interface and thereby protect it from the aggressive electrolyte. The electrochemical analyses confirm that the N-CDs behave as mixed type corrosion inhibitors and protect both anodic and cathodic sites of corrosion. The protective film was chemically analyzed through XPS analysis which confirms the inorganic/organic, hybrid nature of the protective film.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel (MS) is one of the highly used alloys in the industrial sector. The excellent mechanical properties MS within the economic availability makes it a suitable construction material in various infrastructures. Looking at the thermal power industries, MS is widely used for the construction of boilers, boiler tubes, heat exchangers etc. [1–3]. Scaling and rusting are a common problem in these components. The continuous scaling of these components not only lead to delay in regular operations but also decrease the productivity of a power plant. Application of acids such as HCl, HF, H2SO4 etc. to remove these rust and scales is a common practice in these industries [4–7]. These acids effectively dissolve the scales and rust to water soluble metal salts and clear up these boilers for further applications [8–12]. 15 % HCl is one such descaling solution which is widely used in various industries. Application of HCl effectively produces water soluble metal chlorides which are easily cleared up during the cleaning process [13–15]. Application of such aggressive acid solutions also has adverse effects to the metallic surface as well. When the acid solution comes in contact with the metal surface, the metal is also converted to water soluble chlorides and hence the corrosion of the surface begins. Here the acid solution aggressively dissolves the substrate surface and the structural integrity gets compromised. Continuous occurrence of such phenomenon often leads to malfunction, plant shutdowns and in the worst-case loss of human life [16,17]. To counter this situation numerous controlling methods are applied. Amongst these methods, use of corrosion inhibitor is effectively convenient both in efficacy and performance wise. The corrosion inhibitors effectively reduce interaction between the corrosive solution and substrate surface by creating a protective organic or inorganic film over the metallic substrate. This film minimizes the interaction between the corrosive electrolyte and the metal and thereby protect it. It is well reported that inorganic compounds such as molybdates, chromates, vanadates etc. are efficient as corrosion inhibitors but they can have adverse effect upon the locale [18–21]. Considering the environmental concerns, the organic corrosion inhibitors is gaining much importance. In general, organic corrosion inhibitor which generally contain heteroatoms (such as N, S, O) as well as delocalized π electrons are reported as efficient inhibitor in aggressive media [22–27]. It is well reported that aromatic compounds such as triazole [28–30], guanidine [31–33], pyrimidine [34–36], imidazole [37,38], ionic liquids [39–44] etc. effectively reduce corrosion of metal in aqueous acid solution. However, the solubility of these compounds in aqueous condition is often a problem. These organic compounds often separate out in aqueous medium and it takes lot of effort through sonication and heating to prepare a homogeneous mixture. Along with the solubility issues these compounds are often difficult to synthesize through multi-step procedures and purification methodologies [45]. However, carbon dots have come up as an effective alternative to field of organic corrosion inhibitor research. These compounds are reported to be eco-friendly in nature, easily synthesizable through single step reactions. Carbon dots are fluorescent in nature having zero dimension and important properties exhibiting necessary properties for this purpose, such as, low cost, water solubility, easy synthesis methods, environment friendly nature and biocompatibility [46–48]. Doping of heteroatoms is easy method for enhancing the properties of carbon dots. Literature survey reveals nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are good corrosion inhibitors for steel in acidic medium. These N-CDs contain a cluster of graphitic and non-graphitic carbon atoms along with polar functionalities of N and O. These polar functionalities provide them sufficient adsorption capabilities needed for efficient corrosion inhibition performance. The carbon skeletal on the other hand provide an efficient substrate surface coverage [49–51]. The synthesis of carbon dots are often observed to be much simpler than conventional corrosion inhibitors [52]. Majority of the recent literature describe the synthesis through hydrothermal methodologies [53].

Our research group is actively testing the corrosion inhibition application of carbon dots, presenting initial findings focused on understanding and establishing a mechanistic path for the corrosion inhibition capabilities of novel N-CDs. This work represents a pivotal step towards developing highly efficient corrosion inhibitors. A critical observation from existing literature highlights the effectiveness of numerous organic compounds in corrosion inhibition. However, a significant drawback is the multistep synthesis methodology associated with the majority of these compounds. In stark contrast, the N-CDs under scrutiny boast a single-step synthesis method, positioning them as a promising advancement in the quest for highly efficient corrosion inhibitors.

As part of our meticulous methodology, corrosion tests on the starting material did not yield significant resistance against corrosion of Mild Steel in a 15 % HCl solution. Consequently, these results were intentionally excluded from the manuscript, ensuring transparency and precision in our reporting. Similarly, the mixture of materials did not exhibit a noteworthy response in corrosion mitigation, prompting its exclusion from the manuscript. This discerning approach ensures that only the most relevant and impactful findings are presented, consolidating the focus on the potential of N-CDs as corrosion inhibitors.

In comparison to recent literature (Table 1 ), our study offers a detailed investigation into the corrosion inhibition properties of N-CDs, revealing a remarkable 92 % corrosion inhibition at a moderate dosage of 200 ppm. Positioned in the early phases of N-CDs as corrosion inhibitors, this research presents a comprehensive guide for researchers involved in advancing corrosion control methodologies, setting the stage for future breakthroughs in this critical field. For the preparation of investigated N-CDs citric acid, isoniazid and thiourea were taken as the raw material and synthesis was performed by hydrothermal method. Due to many functional groups presents in the raw material, it is anticipated that proposed N-CDs inhibitor can improve adsorption and mitigate the corrosion of mild steel. The N-CDs were characterized by HRTEM, XPS, FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, UV–vis and PL. The weight loss and electrochemical method (EIS and Polarization) were performed to study corrosion inhibition behaviour of inhibitor. For surface study of the sample FESEM, AFM and XPS analysis were performed. Since carbon dots is a novel class of corrosion inhibitors the article represents a study in the early phase of this research. The article represents a much-detailed approach for the researchers working in development of such corrosion inhibitors.

37 % HCl (AR), Rankem; urea, isoniazid, citric acid, Sigma Aldrich. Deionized water was prepared through Merck Millipore, was used for carbon dots (N-CDs) synthesis and corrosive solution preparation.

The investigated MS having configuration: C, 0.31 %; Mn, 0.92 %; P, 0.010 %; S, 0.008 %; Si, 0.19 %; and remaining Fe were utilized for corrosion inhibition studies. The samples used for weight loss study were of the dimensions 6.0 cm × 2.5 cm × 0.1 cm with a hole of 1 mm diameter at the edge for hooking. The samples for electrochemical studies were taken with dimensions of 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 0.1 cm. Each sample was connected with copper wiring in one side to ensure connectivity while the rest are was covered with epoxy resin. The uncovered area was 1 cm2. The respective samples were mechanically abraded and then polished in different grade emery papers to obtain a mirror like polished surface then each sample was degreased in organic solvent (e.g., acetone) and stored in desiccator overnight before each study.

N-CDs were synthesized by Hydrothermal method and the diagram of preparation was displayed in Scheme 1 . Firstly, citric acid (0.38 g, 2 mmol), urea (0.60 g, 10 mmol) and isoniazid (0.411 g, 3 mmol) were dissolved in a beaker containing 20 ml of water and ultrasonicate. Finally, the prepared solution was shifted to Teflon autoclave and put in oven at 180
°C
for 6 h. After the reaction, solution was cooled and transparent product was obtained. The prepared N-CDs carbon dots were characterized by HRTEM, XPS, FTIR, UV–vis spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy and Photoluminescence.

MS coupons were first weighed and submerged in 500 mL acid solution with the help of glass hooks in triplicate in absence and in presence of the inhibitors for a known duration of immersion. The temperatures required for the experiments were maintained in a water circulated thermostat (±0.5 K accuracy). The MS coupons were removed from the acidic solution after the specified time of submersion, splashed in running water and then submerged in cleaning solution. It is finally cleaned with distilled water and dried in stream of warm air before weighing. This process was done triplicate times for accuracy. The surface coverage (θ), rate of corrosion (C R) and inhibition efficiency (η%) were determined by the following equations:

The corrosion study was accomplished by weight loss measurement for different exposure periods ranging for 6–240 h at 303 K to evaluate the effect of exposure period. The effect of change in temperature on weight loss measurement was performed at several temperatures (303, 313, 323, and 333 K) for a period of 6 h.

The electrochemical measurements were conducted on a CH electrochemical workstation (Model No: CHI 660D, manufactured by CH Instruments, Austin, USA) and the three electrodes were the counter electrode, reference electrode and working electrode at 303 K. Prior to perform experiments, the working electrode was submerged in the test solution until a steady open circuit potential (OCP) reached. The potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) is used to determine the electrode voltage of the pole and the modern display is generated as a characteristic of time or power. This method involves changing the static capacity to obtain the necessary data, the correlation drawing and the specific material tension in a particular environment. PDP curves were acquired with a rate of scan of 1 mVs−1 in the range of potential from −700 to −200 mV with respect to OCP. Various electrochemical parameters such as corrosion potential (Ecorr), corrosion current density (icorr), cathodic Tafel slope (β c) and anodic Tafel slope (β a) were obtained through Tafel extrapolation.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a process for analysing the electrode response to corrosion by an AC signal with low power of 10 mV from very wide frequency resistance and in principle, EIS is used to determine kinetic motor parameters associated with factors such as capacitance, inductance and electrical resistance. This measurement was accomplished by using the electrochemical workstation, as stated for polarization measurements in the frequency range from 100 kHz to 10 mHz using amplitude of 10 mV peak to peak AC signal at the OCP. The Nyquist and Bode plots were used for getting impedance data. The EIS spectra for each analysis were examined by using the equivalent circuit in Fig. 12. In equivalent circuit, charge transfer resistance (R t) and the constant phase element (CPE) are in parallel combination and both are in series with the solution resistance (R s). This type of electrochemical equivalent circuit was reported previously to model the iron/acid interface. In this equivalent circuit constant phase element (CPE) is used in the place of pure double layer capacitance (C dl). The reason behind this is that CPE give more exact fit as the double layer at interface does not act as ideal capacitor.

The MS facet was characterized through FESEM, AFM and XPS analysis both before and after corrosion study. These studies were carried out for MS samples of the dimensions 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 0.1 cm. These samples were exposed to pristine and optimum inhibitor containing 15 % HCl solutions. The FESEM analysis was carried out through HITACHI S3400 N and AFM was carried out through Agilent technology 5500. The XPS analysis was carried out for both the N-CDs and corrosion tested samples were carried out using PHI 5000 Versa Probe III spectrometer. The high-resolution spectra for C1s, O1s N1s and Fe2p were processed through XPS Peak-Fit 4.1. The effect of the exposure period was studied through weight loss analysis methodology explained in section 2.3. Here the metal coupons were exposed to the corrosive 15 % HCl solution for different time periods (6 h–240 h) both in presence and absence of the corrosion inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized carbon novel carbon dots (N-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The HR-TEM images of examined inhibitor N-CDs are presented in Fig. 1 . It is observed within the image that the average size of the carbon dots ranges from 10 to 50 nm. The UV–Vis and Photoluminescence analysis was performed to gain knowledge about the optical properties N-CDs. The fluorescent nature of the prepared N-CDs was confirmed through fluorescence spectrum presented in Fig. 2 a. The fluorescence spectrum displaying the maximum emission at 445 nm. The trapping of excited energy from the passivated surface heteroatoms outcome in the absorption, by which may give strong fluorescence.

The UV absorption spectrum as presented in Fig. 2b exhibit two apparent absorption bands. The strong absorption peak at 267 nm was associated to the π-π* transition and the broad but weak absorption band at 340 nm was ascribed to the n- π* CN/CO bond [53].

To characterize chemical composition and structure of prepared carbon dots, FTIR spectra was taken. Fig. 2c displayed the FTIR spectra of synthesized N-CDs. The FTIR spectra of prepared dots shows a broad peak at 3450 was stretching vibration of N–H and O–H, while the peak at 1629 was related to CO. Peaks at 1395 and 1574 are allotted to the C–N and CN respectively.

To get the information about different state of carbon, Raman spectra was measured. Fig. 3 (a) shows the Raman spectra of as prepared N-CDs, contains two broad bands at 1450 and 1560 cm−1 could be associated to the D and G bands, respectively. The G band associated with the sp2 bonded carbon atoms and D band is related to sp3 bonded carbon atoms. The ID/IG ratio for N-CDs is 1.23, which indicates the presence of sp3 hybridized caron moieties in a larger extent in comparison to sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The Raman spectrum clarifies the presence of both sp3 and sp2 C atoms within the structural moiety of the examined inhibitor N-CDs [74].

The Further characterization of the prepared dots were carried out by XPS study. The high-resolution XPS spectra of these elements are presented in Fig. 3. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) reveal the presence of CO functionality at 287.58 eV, C–O/C–N functionalities at 285.47 eV and carbonaceous functionalities at 284.26 eV. The presence of these groups supports the findings of Raman spectra. The oxygen functionalities present within the carbon dots are confirmed thorough the deconvoluted O1s spectrum presented in Fig. 3c. The spectrum is deconvoluted into two peaks at 530.82 eV and 532.31 eV of CO and C–OH, respectively. The N 1s spectra is presented in Fig. 3d were deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.88 eV, 399.99 eV, and 400.84 eV; representing N–H, C–N, CN respectively [53,54,75]. The XPS analysis reveal the atomic percentages of C, 60.70 %; O, 21.70 % and N, 17.51 %.

The overall characterization of the N-CDs reveals the presence of functionalized N along with C and O. C being the fundamental structural framework of the N-CDs has the highest atomic percentage, while the O and N functionalities exists as N–H, C–N, CN, O–H and CO functional groups. These functionalities indicate towards the presence of N atoms doped within the structural carbonaceous moiety, while the O atoms exists predominantly as functional groups only.

The weight loss analysis of MS coupons was carried out in 15 % HCl in presence and absence N-CDs as corrosion inhibitors within the temperature range of 313K–333K. The evaluated corrosion parameters are presented as data in Table 2 and graphically in Fig. 4 . It observed that the apparent rate of corrosion (C R) decreased with the increase in corrosion inhibitor dosage. As presented in Table 2, the value of C R was observed to be 38.4 mmy−1 for MS coupons in pristine 15 % HCl at 303 K. The C R values were however noticed to reduce up to 2.65 mmy−1 for a dosage of 350 ppm. The corrosion inhibition efficiency also followed similar trend and an efficiency of 92.00 % was observed for a dosage of 200 ppm. Beyond this dosage of the N-CDs, so significant change in corrosion ƞ% was noticed and thus the concentration was acknowledged as the optimum concentration for the investigation. This observation refers to the fact that the majority of the corrosion active sites upon the substrate surface has been protected due to corrosion mitigative action of the N-CDs. Since majority of the active sites have been protected through adsorption of the corrosion inhibitors, no significant change has been observed beyond this dosage [76]. This mild steel inhibition with addition of studied inhibitor could be ascribed to the protective film formation of examined inhibitor (N-CDs) on MS facet [43], which blocks the metals active site and thus do not let the corrosion occur. N-CDs can make adsorption film by the donating lone pair of nitrogen to the vacant orbital of iron present in MS. The increment in ƞ% with increment on inhibitor concentration indicates that a greater number of N-CDs molecules adsorbed on MS surface leading to the formation of strong protective film. The decrement in ƞ% with increment in temperature suggested the desorption of some adsorbed N-CDs molecules from MS surface. The corrosion inhibition performance however followed the reverse trend with the increase in temperature and it was observed to reduce with rise in temperature for all the studied dosages. The corrosion inhibition performance was observed to reduce to 84.80 % at 333K. The decrease in ƞ% is ascribed to dissolution of the protective thin film generated due to adsorption of N-CDs upon the MS facet. The gradual dissolution of the protective film upon the MS surface allows the exposure of the substate to the corrosive electrolyte and thereby enhance the corrosion rate. This refers to the decrease in corrosion inhibition efficiency as observed within the experimental data [77,78].

The effect of exposure period was studied for the optimum concentration of 200 ppm at the temperature of 303 K for a wide range of time periods ranging up to 240 h. The obtained data is displayed in Table 3 and Fig. 5 . The obtained data reflect the decrease in corrosion inhibition efficiency (η%) with exposure period. The values of η% were observed to decrease rapidly up to the exposure period of 48 h. The observed value of η% was 92.47 % after 6 h and 85.50 % after 48 h. This observation can be attributed to the dissolution of metal from the unprotected sites upon the MS surface. The rate dissolution of metal from these sites is higher in comparison to the adsorption of the N-CDs upon the MS surface. Hence the rapid decrease is observed up to 48 h of exposure. Beyond the exposure period of 48 h the decrease of η% is much less. This occurrence is attributed to one of the two factors; firstly, the apparent process of corrosion has attained equilibrium after the mentioned exposure period or the aggressive nature of the acid solution has been compromised due to dissolution of metal salts within the acidic electrolyte. However, it is strongly affirmed by the obtained data that the N-CDs are efficient corrosion inhibitors for prolonged exposure period as it is able to sustain high values of η% (>80 %) after prolonged exposure of 240 h.

MS corrosion was calculated in the temperature ranging from 303 to 333K with and without N-CDs. Corrosion rate logarithm (log C R) versus absolute temperature reciprocal was displayed in Fig. 5. The obtained plots are linear in nature it suggested that it follows Arrhenius equation, given by relation presented in equation (7).

The Ea values noted in Table 4 were evaluated from the slope of liner plot Fig. 6 a. The Ea values are noticed to enhance with enhancement in N-CDs dosage. The enhancement in the values of Ea indicates the rise in Ea of the corrosion process. This indicates the slowing of the corrosion process in presence of the N-CDs. N-CDs as compared in absence of prepared CDs suggested the N-CDs adsorption on MS facet, reduces with enhancement in temperature and the decrement in process of adsorption of investigated dots on the MS facet with enhancement in temperature and enhancement in rate of reaction of a larger area of metal that is exposed to aggressive media. Enthalpy of adsorption (ΔH a) and entropy of adsorption (ΔS a) for MS with and without N-CDs were acquired by using transition state equation (8).

Plot of log (C
R /T) vs 1000/T were made as reflected in Fig. 6b. The obtained plot is linear in nature and ΔH a and ΔS a were evaluated by the slope and intercept of the linear plot, respectively and data is noted in Table 4. The values of ΔH a enhances when studied inhibitor was added, it shows physical adsorption and positive value of ΔH a for free acid solution as well as with N-CDs discloses that inhibitor presence does not affect the corrosion analysis.

The ΔS a value were noticed to be negative, it reveals the association of molecules of inhibitor on MS facet during rate determining step and it is also reflected from Table 4 that ΔS a values increases with increment in concentration of studied inhibitor, indicates the removal of adsorbed water molecules from the experimental metal facet during the adsorption of a greater number of water molecules by N-CDs single molecules. Thermodynamic and activation data inferred that enhancement in N-CDs concentration, metal dissolution activation energy enhances. It recommends that corrosion action delays in the presence of examined inhibitor. Hence, it shows the adsorption of the molecules of inhibitor on MS facet [76].

To demonstrate the interaction of studied corrosion inhibitor and metal substrate, adsorption isotherm was used. After careful literature survey as presented in Table 1 it is evident that majority of the reported quantum dots display best fitting to Langmuir adsorption isotherm. However, some other isotherms have also been reported to display effective fitting. In order to obtain a comprehensive understanding about the adsorption nature of the studied N-CDs 6 different isotherms were tried against the obtained. The tried isotherms are represented in equations (9)–(14), while the plotted isotherms are presented in supplementary information.

Here the term C inh is the concentration of the inhibitor in gL−1, θ is the surface area coverage obtained through weight loss study and K ads is the equilibrium constant for adsorption-desorption process.

Fig. 7 represents the fitted data against Langmuir isotherm which display the best fitting amongst the isotherms. The isotherms were used to calculate the value of K ads, which was in turn used to calculate the free energy of adsorption (ΔG ads) using the following equation:
(15)

G ads
= −
R T
ln
(
1000 ×
K ads
)
Where R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J K− 1mol−1), T is the temperature (K) and 1000 gL-1 is the concentration of water. The values of ΔG ads are observed to be −29.37 kJmol-1 at 303 K, −30.56 kJmol-1 at 313 K, −31.02 kJmol-1 at 323 K and −32.31 kJmol-1 at 333 K. All the values of ΔG ads lie within the range of −20 kJmol-1 and -40 kJmol-1. This indicates that the N-CDs interact with the MS surface through both chemical and electrostatic interactions [79–81].

OCP is the working electrode potential in equilibrium with the corrosive electrolyte within the cell setup. The OCP analysis was carried out prior to every electrochemical study both in pristine and N-CDs containing 15 % HCl. The OCP was recorded for a time period of 3600 s and it is represented in Fig. 8 a. It is evident form each OCP that the values were nearly constant after 1800 s. The OCP for MS in pristine 15 % HCl was noticed to be −0.494 V (vs SCE). The OCP with corrosion inhibitors fall well within the set of ±0.085 V. The maximum deviation was noticed to be 0.019 V (vs SCE) for a dosage of 150 ppm. This observation suggests that the studied N-CDs behave as anodic as well as cathodic (mixed type) corrosion inhibitors [82–84].

Tafel curve of MS steel in several concentration of studied inhibitor was displayed in Fig. 8 b and Table 5 contain their respective parameters such as E Corr, β a, β c and i corr were noted, and ƞ% was determined by equation (4). It is often assumed that the rate of charge transfer at the electrode electrolyte interface is much slower than that of mass transfer. Hence the dissolution of the metal occurs at much faster rate in comparison to the hydrogen evaluation reaction. In the experimentally obtained data presented in Table 5 it is evident that both the values of βa and βc has increased with the increment of dosage corrosion inhibitors; N-CDs. Both the values have increased significantly and it is evident that the N-CDs have interfered with the aforementioned corrosion mechanism. The increased values of βa refer to control in anodic metal dissolution process upon the electrode surface. While the increased value of βc indicates towards suppression of cathodic reduction reactions. This change in the corrosion mechanism is attributed to the adsorption of the N-CDs molecules over the MS surface [85–88]. The values of corrosion potential shifted in the direction of negative potential with increment in concentration of inhibitor. In terms of i corr values, reduction was seen after introducing N-CDs, it may be ascribed to the accretion of investigated inhibitor on MS facet. Tafel curves after introduction of N-CDs are same as compared to the blank, this discloses that mechanism of corrosion does not influence after introducing N-CDs. The arc of both anode and cathode migrated towards the lesser value of i corr, demonstrate the physical as well as chemical adsorption of investigated inhibitor. The anodic and cathodic slope values as displayed in Table 6 are nearly same after introducing N- CDs, reports that corrosion process does not alter after introducing investigated inhibitor.

The E Corr values after introduction of investigated inhibitor in acidic medium are lower than ±85 mV to the blank, suggested that investigated N- CDs is mixed type inhibitor. After introducing N- CDs, measured values of corrosion potential moved slightly in the positive direction as comparison to the values before introducing investigated inhibitor, from this it can be demonstrated that investigated inhibitor behave as a mix type but mainly anodic type reaction. The small difference in the value of βa and βc after introducing investigated inhibitor, revealed that investigated inhibitor controlled both reactions i.e., anode and cathode. The efficiency of N- CDs were ranging from 71.9 % at 15 ppm to 90.1 % at 200 ppm. Therefore, it can be stated that protective and stable film of N- CDs formed, thereby forming a barrier against the aggressive attack of HCl to give inhibitive ability for metal corrosion in 15 % HCl solution.

The Nyquist plot of MS in corrosive solution with numerous dosages of N-CDs is presented in Fig. 11 (a). It is apparent from the Figure that Nyquist plot reveal single capacitive depressed semicircle with a small inductive loop in absence of the corrosion inhibitor. The high and low frequency regions of the depressed semi-circle indicates that corrosion process is charge transfer controlled in pristine 15 % HCl. The obtained nyquist plots consists of the charge transfer resistance (R t), the resistance occurring at metal/electrolyte interface (R a) and diffuse later resistance (R L). Upon ignoring the low frequency inductive loop, the polarization resistance R P is given as summation of R t, R a and R L [89,90]. The capacitive semi-circle loop deviates from an ideal capacitor due to the inhomogeneity at the MS electrode surface. The increment was seen in diameter of capacitive loop after introducing N-CDs. This consistent increment in diameter of capacitive loop reveals the adsorption of N-CDs molecules on the facet of MS. This thin film which was made by the molecules of N-CDs reduces the direct exposure of the MS, enhancement in nature of corrosion resistance and remarkably prevents the MS corrosion in aggressive solution. The equivalent circuit, showed in Fig. 9 was utilized for fitting the experimental data containing of R s, R p and CPE. For better fitting of data of experiment CPE was used in circuit in the place of C dl. equations (5) and (6) were used to evaluate the corrosion ƞ % and the C dl.

It is clearly visible from Fig. 9 a. theoretical data and experimental data are in good correlation, representing that the circuit which is displayed in Fig. 10
is suitable for the fitting of Nyquist plots. The EIS parameter is noted in Table 6, From EIS data, it is visible that increment in R p values enhances with enhancement in N-CDs concentration, recommending the formation of hindrance layer of studied carbon dots on facet of MS. The decrement in C dl values with introducing N-CDs, with enhancement in dosage of inhibitors demonstrated that the studied carbon dots (N-CDs) formed hindrance layer on MS facet through replacing molecules of water from metal interface, leads to the decrement in metal dissolution rate. Thus, increment in efficiency with increment in dosage of studied carbon dots (N-CDs) reaching up to 73.6 for 15 ppm, 92.5 for 200 ppm at 303 K.

Fig. 9b represents the Bode plots. Only one peak was seen in Bode plots for N-CDs disclose the existence of single time constant as revealed in Nyquist plots. The increment in impedance modulus over whole frequency range enhances enhancement in dosage of N-CDs. Also, the frequency range with the maximum phase angle rises as the dosage of examined carbon dots increases. These outcomes recommended the adsorption of N-CDs molecules on substrate facet [91].

PZC was calculate using EIS spectroscopy. The PZC analysis provided the nature of charge at the MS surface within the corrosive environment. This analysis was carried out for pristine and optimum concentration NCDs containing 15 % HCl. The evaluated values of C dl were plotted against potential to acquire the parabolic curves displayed in Fig. 9d. The Antopov's rational potential value (E
A ) was evaluated to know the charge nature upon the MS facet by using equation (16) [92,93].
16
E A
=
E
o c p
−
E
p z c

The EA vales for blank were observed to be 6 mV for blank and 9 mV for the solution containing N-CDs. The positive values indicate that the metal facet is positively charged in 15 % HCl solution. The Cl− ions then adsorb upon this surface to generate the negatively surface. Upon this surface the –NH3 + functionalities adsorb to generate a mixed organic/inorganic layer to protect the surface.

The MS surface was characterized through FESEM, EDX and AFM analyses. These analyses are embodied in Fig. 11. The FESEM analysis reveal a very smooth MS surface with marks of polishing, Fig. 11a. The degradation of the polished MS surface is clearly observed in presence of pristine corrosive solution, Fig. 11b. The protection of the MS surface is however confirmed in Fig. 11c, which display much lesser number of cracks and pits than the previous image. The EDX analysis (Fig. 11 d, e, f) disclose the occurrence of respective elements upon the MS surface. The polished MS surface, Fig. 11d disclose the presence of Fe and C, the intrinsic components of the alloy. The MS surface exposed to pristine corrosive solution; Fig. 11e disclose the presence of Cl along with the elements in the previous image. The presence of Cl is indicative of the chloride salts produced during the process of corrosion. The EDX spectrum of the MS surface expose to optimum concentration of NCDs (Fig. 11f) containing 15 % HCl is observed to contain Fe, C, N, and O. The presence of these elements further indicates the N-CDs adsorption upon the MS surface.

The surface topography of the MS surfaces was observed through AFM analysis. The polished MS surface display a smooth topography with marks of polishing; Fig. 11g. The MS surface exposed to pristine acidic solution show the existence of a greater number of cracks and pits due to aggressive action of the corrosive electrolyte; Fig. 11h. The surface to N-CDs containing 15 % HCl (Fig. 11i) on the other hand display much lesser number of cracks and pits. It can be inferred from these analyses that the MS surface is rigorously corroded in presence of pristine 15 % HCl. The application of the N-CDs significantly protects the substrate surface from further corrosion.

To get the detailed understanding about the nature of adsorption film established on the facet of MS, XPS analysis was performed. The survey scan of pristine N-CDs and MS surface exposed to N-CDs containg 15 % HCl is presented in Fig. 12. The image reflects the absence of Fe2+ within the N-CDs while the presence of all the elements of the corrosion inhibitors upon the MS surface. Fig. 15 displayed the deconvoluted spectra of C1s, N 1s, O 1s and Fe 2p of inhibited N-CDs. The high resolution C1s spectra displayed in Fig. 12 (a) could be divided into three peaks of 284.42 eV, 285.78 eV, 287.91 eV, were associated to C–C/CC, C–N/CN, CO, respectively. For N 1s spectra nitrogen signals at 399.71 eV, 400.78 eV and 401.65 eV are ascribed to C–N, N–Fe and N+-H, respectively.

The characteristics peaks of oxygen of Fe2O3 at 530.63 eV, CO at 531.70 eV, and C–OH at 532.90 eV were observed in Fig. 13 (b). The Fe2p3/2 (Fig. 13 (d)), could be divided into four peaks, peak at 709.55 eV allotted to metallic iron. The peak at 711.46 eV and 714.53 eV could be ascribed to Fe2+ such as Fe3O4 and Fe3+ for ferric compounds such as Fe2O3 and FeOOH.

The presence of the organic C1s, N1s and O1s elements along with inorganic Fe and O1s components clearly indicate the formation of an inorganic and organic hybrid film at the MS-15 % HCl interface and thereby confirm the corrosion mitigation.

Contact angle analysis was accomplished to confirm the establishment of layer of protection on the surface of MS. In this analysis a droplet of 4 μl of distilled water was plunged on MS facet, after deposition of the drop of distilled water on MS facet, angle of contact was measured. Fig. 14 (a), (b) and (c) shows the pictures of measurement of contact angle of polished specimen, specimen dipped in corrosive solution and specimen dipped with N-CDs. The angle of contact of polished specimen (Fig. 14 (a)) was found at 72.7°. But when mild steel specimen was dipped in acid solution, the angle was reduced to 26.7° (Fig. 14 (b)). In presence of inhibitor (N-CDs), angle was increased to 84.2
°
(Fig. 16 (c)). Decrease in wettability and increase in hydrophobic nature suggested the establishment of protective film of N-CDs on MS facet.

The N-CDs environment friendly, water-soluble corrosion inhibitor has shown good performance in mitigation of corrosion for MS in 15 % hydrochloric acid. Hydrothermal technique was used for the preparation of studied carbon dots and their characterization was accomplished by HRTEM, PL, FTIR, XPS and UV–vis. The η% of N-CDs at 200 ppm of concentration is 92.00 at 303 K. The η% was enhanced with enhancement in the concentration of N-CDs and reduces with increment in temperature due to the desorption of molecules of studied inhibitor (N-CDs) from the MS surface. The N-CDs obey Langmuir adsorption isotherm. On the basis of Potentiodynamic measurement studied inhibitor is mixed type in nature. EIS studies shows that charge transfer resistance increases with increment in concentration and reduction in C dl with increment in concentration, suggesting the establishment of thin layer of N-CDs on MS facet. FESEM, AFM and XPS validates the formation of film of protection on MS surface.

Vandana Saraswat: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Tarun Kanti Sarkar: Formal analysis, Manuscript writing. Mahendra Yadav: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
